title: The Flood That Brought Us Together
body:
**Where did you grow up? What was it like?**
I grew up in a small town outside of Houston. It was quiet, lots of open fields, the kind of place where everyone knew your name. My parents ran a little grocery store and I spent most of my childhood helping them stock shelves and sweep floors.

**How did you end up living here in this neighborhood?**
My husband got transferred to Katy for work back in 2003. We drove around for weeks looking at neighborhoods and the moment we turned onto the streets of Raintree Village, I told him this was it. The trees, the way the houses sat back from the road, it just felt like home.

**What moment in your life changed you the most?**
Harvey. When the water started rising in 2017, I thought we were going to lose everything. But then our neighbors showed up. People I had only waved to from the driveway were suddenly in my living room helping us move furniture upstairs. Tom from three doors down brought his boat. The Nguyen family took in six families. I had lived here for fourteen years and thought I knew this neighborhood, but I didn't really know it until that week.

**What are you most proud of?**
After Harvey, I helped start a neighborhood emergency preparedness group. We meet every quarter now. We have a phone tree, supply kits, the whole thing. I'm proud that something terrible turned into something that made us stronger.

title: Thirty Years on Raintree Lane
body:
**Where did you grow up? What was it like?**
Right here in Katy. I'm one of the originals. My parents moved to Raintree Village in 1994 when I was eight years old. Back then half the lots were still dirt. I remember watching them build the houses on our street one by one.

**How did you end up living here in this neighborhood?**
I never really left. Went to college at UH, came back, and when a house two streets over from my parents went on the market in 2012, my wife and I jumped on it. My kids now ride their bikes on the same sidewalks I used to ride on.

**Who influenced your life the most?**
Mr. Patterson, who lived on the corner of Raintree and Willow. He was a retired engineer and he used to let all the neighborhood kids come into his garage and build things. Birdhouses, go-karts, you name it. He passed away in 2019 but I still think about him every time I drive past that house.

**What makes a good life in your opinion?**
Staying close to the people who matter. I know that sounds simple but I've watched a lot of people chase careers and money to the other side of the country. I chose to stay where my roots are. My kids know their grandparents. They know their neighbors. That's worth more than any salary.

title: The Day I Became an American
body:
**Tell us about a meaningful story or moment from your life.**
On March 14, 2018, I became a United States citizen. I had been living in Raintree Village for eleven years by then, raising my two daughters, working at the elementary school as a teaching aide. But that day changed everything.

I remember coming home from the ceremony in downtown Houston, still holding my little American flag, and my neighbor Linda had put a banner on my front door that said CONGRATULATIONS ROSA. I didn't even know she knew. But somehow the whole street found out.

That evening, people kept stopping by. Someone brought a cake. The Rodriguez family brought tamales. My daughters were so proud. My oldest said, Mom, you're finally official. I told her, I've been official since the day we moved here.

This neighborhood made me feel American long before any piece of paper did. The way people included us, invited us to block parties, helped us when our car broke down. That is what citizenship really means to me.

title: We Raised Four Kids on This Street
body:
**Where did you grow up? What was it like?**
Patricia: I grew up in Louisiana, in a big family. Seven kids. We didn't have much but we had each other. James grew up right here in Texas, military family, moved around a lot.

James: That's why when we found Raintree Village, I told Pat, we're not moving again. This is it.

**What was your first job?**
James: I was a welder at a shipyard in Galveston. Hardest work I ever did. Patricia worked at a bank downtown for twenty-two years.

Patricia: And raised four children while doing it, thank you very much.

**What was the hardest period in your life?**
James: When our youngest, Michael, was diagnosed with leukemia in 2009. He was only six. The neighborhood organized meal trains, drove our other kids to school, even helped with medical bills. He's healthy now, twenty-three years old and in graduate school. But we will never forget what these people did for our family.

**What advice would you give someone younger than you?**
Patricia: Invest in your neighbors. Not money, time. Learn their names. Bring them food when they're sick. Watch their house when they travel. It comes back to you tenfold. We've been here twenty-six years and these people are our family.

title: The Garden That Fed the Block
body:
**Tell us about a meaningful story or moment from your life.**
When COVID hit in 2020, I lost my job at a restaurant in the Galleria. I was scared. I had savings for maybe two months. But I also had a big backyard and twenty years of gardening experience from growing up on my grandmother's farm in Vietnam.

So I started growing food. Tomatoes, peppers, herbs, bok choy, green beans. Within a few weeks I had more than my family could eat. I started leaving bags of vegetables on my neighbors' doorsteps.

Pretty soon people started asking if they could help. The Johnsons gave me extra soil. Maria across the street brought seeds from her mother's garden in Mexico. David's kids came over to learn how to plant.

By summer we had a full community garden running out of three backyards on our street. We grew over two hundred pounds of food that year. Some of it went to the food bank.

The restaurant eventually called me back but I kept the garden going. Five years later it's still here. Every Saturday morning you'll find us out there, hands in the dirt, talking about life. It started because I was desperate. It survived because people showed up.
